1:
The chapter opens on a change of throne, and the witnesses divide their labour between fixing the date and weighing the man. The grammatical-historical commentators settle the chronology. Charles Ellicott notes that ‘began to reign’ is, literally, simply ‘reigned,’ and cross-checks the synchronism against the notes at 2 Kings 1:17 and 2 Kings 8:16. John Gill works the arithmetic: since Jehoram accedes in Jehoshaphat’s eighteenth year while Ahaziah had begun in the seventeenth, the ‘two years’ of Ahaziah’s reign were not two full years but the close of one and the opening of another, and Jehoram himself will reign twelve years. Adam Clarke stands back and gives the whole chapter its argument in a sentence — the idolatry of Jehoram, the revolt of Mesha, the allied march that nearly perishes for thirst, Elisha’s oracle of water and victory, and the grim close upon the wall of Kir-haraseth.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the moral portrait, and it is a study in half-measures. Bad Jehoram was, Henry grants, ‘yet not like his father Ahab or his mother Jezebel’; two things stand to his credit — he put away the image of Baal, and he bestirred himself to recover what his brother had lost. Henry even guesses that Jehoshaphat’s alliance, which made his own house worse, may have done something toward making Ahab’s better. Yet the reformation, he says, ‘was next to none’: Jehoram removed Baal’s image but did not root out Baal from the people, whom Jehu later found still swarming with his worship (2 Kings 10:19); and, tellingly, he clung to the calves of Jeroboam.

Henry’s most searching stroke is on the logic of a self-interested repentance. Jehoram, he observes, learned to fear Baal by seeing his father and brother ‘cut off for worshipping Baal,’ yet even his purge came late enough that the destruction of Ahab’s house fell in his own days and upon his own person (2 Kings 9:24), when ‘the measure of its iniquity was full.’ Those, Henry lands, ‘do not truly repent or reform, who only part with the sins that they lose by, but continue their affection to the sins that they get by’ — the calves being kept, as Jehoram saw them, as a ‘state engine’ to hold the two kingdoms apart. He was not, in Solomon’s phrase, ‘overmuch wicked’ (Eccl. 7:17); but a king who reforms his household and not his kingdom, and who lays Baal’s image aside without breaking it in pieces, has done a thing ‘well’ that ‘was not enough.’ The voices agree: the reign begins with a man better than his parents and far short of his God.
2:
Here the narrator strikes his careful balance — Jehoram ‘wrought evil,’ yet ‘not like his father, and like his mother’ — and the witnesses probe both halves. John Gill puts the charge in a word: he ‘was guilty of idolatry.’ Adam Clarke draws the line the verse itself draws: ‘he abolished his worship’ of Baal ‘but he continued that of the calves at Dan and Beth-el.’ The reform was real but partial, a turning from the imported cult of Baal while the old schism-worship of the calves stood untouched, as the next verse (v. 3) will press.

The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the political and textual detail that makes the half-measure intelligible. Charles Ellicott observes that ‘wrought evil’ means he ‘did the evil in the eyes’ of the Lord — that is, he maintained the illicit bullock-worship at Bethel — while the added clause ‘like his mother’ is pointed: Jezebel ‘lived throughout his reign’ (2 Kings 9:30), which explains why he could not, or would not, eradicate the Baal-worship that Jehu would finally stamp out (2 Kings 10:18-28). The ‘image’ he ‘put away,’ Ellicott argues, is better rendered a ‘pillar,’ removed from its place in the temple of Baal that Ahab had built (1 Kings 16:31-32); and since it plainly reappears to be destroyed later (2 Kings 10:26-27), it must have been restored, ‘probably by the influence of Jezebel.’ He even notes that the Septuagint and Vulgate read the plural ‘pillars’ and add ‘and brake them in pieces,’ which he thinks ‘seems original,’ Ahab being likely to have set up more than one (compare 2 Chronicles 34:4).

Keil and Delitzsch keep their eye on the seam between the reign’s opening and the war to come, reading the removal of Baal’s image as the one bright note before the narrative turns to Moab’s revolt and the tribute of lambs and rams. Taken together the voices refuse to flatter Jehoram and refuse to blacken him beyond the text: he broke with the worst of his mother’s imports, and yet his heart, and his mother, remained. The synthesis the witnesses reach is the sober one — a reform that removes a pillar but leaves the calves, and leaves Jezebel, is a reform that heaven still records as ‘evil in the sight of the Lord.’ It is the portrait of a man who will do just enough to be better than Ahab and never enough to be right with God, so that even his one good deed had, in Ellicott’s telling, to be done over again by a bloodier hand.
3:
This verse is the hinge of Jehoram’s character, and though the witnesses are few and brief, they converge on a single, penetrating diagnosis: what he kept tells more than what he removed. The word is ‘nevertheless.’ Having put away Baal’s pillar (v. 2), ‘nevertheless he cleaved unto the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, which made Israel to sin; he departed not therefrom.’ John Gill states it plainly — ‘he closely adhered to the worship of the calves set up by’ Jeroboam — and the narrator’s verb ‘cleaved’ carries all the weight of a settled, deliberate attachment.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott anchors the phrase in its history, pointing back to Jeroboam’s founding apostasy (1 Kings 12:28) and to the standing indictment of the northern kings (1 Kings 16:26), and he notes a grammatical nicety — the ‘therefrom’ is Hebrew ‘from it,’ a collective feminine, echoed in the later refrains over Jehoahaz and Jehoash (2 Kings 13:2). It is the formula by which the historian measures king after king against the original sin of the divided kingdom, and Jehoram, for all his reform, falls under it.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley supplies the motive the bare notice leaves unspoken, and it is the sharpest word on the verse. ‘The sins,’ he explains, were ‘the worship of the calves, which all the kings of Israel kept up as a wall of partition between their subjects and those of Judah.’ Here is the reason a man who feared to keep Baal would not let go the calves: they were not devotion but policy, the state’s guarantee that Israel’s people would not drift south to worship at Jerusalem and so back toward the house of David. Wesley’s summary is devastating in its restraint: ‘although he had a little religion, yet he had not enough to over-rule this policy.’ That is the whole tragedy of Jehoram in a sentence. Religion enough to remove an idol his heart had cooled toward; not religion enough to surrender the idol his throne leaned on. The witnesses do not debate here; they agree that a faith which reforms only where reform costs nothing, and clings wherever the crown has an interest, is a faith already mastered by the very kingdom it means to serve — a lesson that reaches past Samaria to every half-hearted repentance that keeps back the sin it profits by.
4:
The narrative now backs up to name the rebel and reckon his wealth, and the witnesses turn philologists and historians together. Mesha ‘was a sheepmaster,’ and rendered to Israel ‘an hundred thousand lambs, and an hundred thousand rams, with the wool.’ John Gill reads the plain sense — a man whose ‘substance chiefly consisted’ in flocks, who ‘traded in them, and got great riches by them.’

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott brings the whole apparatus of the word to bear. The name Mesha, he notes, means ‘deliverance, salvation,’ and — strikingly — ‘occurs on the monument set up by this king,’ the Moabite Stone that records his own version of these very wars. The term rendered ‘sheepmaster’ is the rare Hebrew noqed, used elsewhere only of the prophet Amos (Amos 1:1), and cognate to an Arabic word for a superior breed of wool-bearing sheep and its owner. Ellicott paints the country: Moab is ‘mountainous, but well watered, and rich in fertile valleys,’ land ‘specially suited for pasture,’ with the Arabian wilderness open to its shepherds. The tribute, he insists, was ‘paid in kind’ and ‘consisted of a hundred thousand fatted lambs and the fleeces of a hundred thousand rams’ — the phrase ‘with the wool’ attaching properly only to the rams — and he calls it ‘a heavy burden for a country no larger than the county of Huntingdon,’ an oppression Mesha himself complains of on his stone (compare the prophecy of Moab’s lamb-tribute in Isaiah 16:1).

The wesleyan-arminian voices attend to the same facts with a different accent. Adam Clarke presses the word noqed toward the practice of marking or branding sheep — one ‘who marks or brands, probably from the marking of sheep’ — a man with so many flocks that each bore his particular sign of ownership; and he records the curious variant of the Chaldee and Arabic, which for ‘a hundred thousand lambs’ read ‘a hundred thousand fat oxen.’ John Wesley draws the line from wealth to war: a ‘sheepmaster’ is ‘a man of great wealth (which in those times and places consisted much in cattle) which enabled and emboldened him to rebel against his sovereign.’

Wesley’s note is the thread that binds the verse to the story. The genealogy of the revolt runs through the flocks: it was riches, and the confidence riches breed, that let a vassal defy his overlord. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the historian pauses over Moab’s sheep not for antiquarian interest but to show the sinews of rebellion, and that the same tribute which had enriched Israel’s crown became, when withheld, the occasion of the whole campaign that follows the death of Ahab (2 Kings 1:1).
5:
The historian repeats a fact he has already given (2 Kings 1:1) — ‘when Ahab was dead, the king of Moab rebelled against the king of Israel’ — and the witnesses ask why it is told twice and what the vassal’s ‘rebellion’ amounted to. Charles Ellicott answers the second question first: to ‘rebel’ here is simply to refuse ‘payment of the annual tribute,’ and he notes the textual wrinkle that where some manuscripts read ‘when Ahab was dead,’ the ordinary text has ‘about the time of Ahab’s death,’ a small shift of one Hebrew particle.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the repetition as the seam of the narrative: the note of 2 Kings 1:1 ‘is repeated here, because it furnished the occasion for the expedition about to be described.’ The revolt had stood unpunished through the brief, ailing reign of Ahaziah, who ‘had been unable to do anything during his short reign to renew the subjugation of Moab’; and so ‘Joram was therefore anxious to overtake what had been neglected immediately after his ascent of the throne.’ They picture the sequence exactly — the king demands the tribute afresh, Moab refuses, and ‘at that time’ Jehoram goes to Samaria, ‘raised an army out of the whole kingdom,’ and requests Jehoshaphat’s help, as Ahab once had (1 Kings 22:4). They add the note of conscience that hangs over Judah’s king: he joined ‘notwithstanding the fact that he had been blamed by prophets for his alliance with Ahab and Ahaziah’ (2 Chronicles 19:2; 2 Chronicles 20:37), probably because he ‘wished to chastise the Moabites still further’ for their late invasion of his own land (2 Chronicles 20).

The reformed John Gill turns the same facts on the character of the dead Ahaziah, who ‘either through the pusillanimity of his temper, or the sickness that attended him, or the shortness of his reign, took no steps to the reduction’ of Moab — a passivity that let a withheld tribute harden into an established revolt. Between them the witnesses give the verse its function: it is the historian’s deliberate backward glance, marking that the debt has been outstanding since Ahab’s death, that a weak reign let it fester, and that a new and more vigorous king means to collect it. The stage is set not by ambition alone but by an old neglect, and by a godly king of Judah once more binding himself to the house of Ahab against the counsel of the prophets — a fateful alliance the chapter will not let us forget.
6:
With a single verb the war begins: ‘king Jehoram went out of Samaria the same time, and numbered all Israel.’ Charles Ellicott renders ‘numbered’ as ‘mustered, made a levy of,’ and reads ‘the same time’ as the Hebrew ‘in that day’ — less definite than the English, meaning the moment ‘when the Moabite refusal of tribute was received by Jehoram, who, on his accession, would demand it afresh.’ John Gill agrees the muster followed hard upon the accession, ‘as soon as he came to the throne.’

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the bare notice into reflection. ‘Jehoram has no sooner got the sceptre into his hand than he takes the sword into his hand.’ Crowns, he moralizes, ‘bring great cares and perils to the heads that wear them; no sooner in honour than in war.’ Then he watches the making of the alliance with a shrewd eye. Jehoram ‘courted’ Jehoshaphat — ‘Wilt thou go with me against Moab?’ — and gained him, the king of Judah answering, ‘I am as thou art’ (v. 7). Henry admires that ‘Judah and Israel, though unhappily divided from each other, yet can unite against Moab a common enemy,’ and that Jehoshaphat ‘upbraids them not with their revolt from the house of David,’ treating Israel ‘as a sister-kingdom.’ Those, he adds, ‘are no friends to their own peace and strength who can never find in their hearts to forgive and forget an old injury.’

Yet Henry will not let the generosity pass without warning, and here his voice sharpens toward the chapter’s deepest note. Jehoshaphat ‘had like to have paid dearly for joining with Ahab, yet he joined with his son, and this expedition also had like to have been fatal to him. There is nothing got by being yoked with unbelievers.’ The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the plain history beneath the moral: Jehoram ‘went and sent to Jehoshaphat,’ who ‘willingly promised’ as he once had to Ahab, and the two kings then debated the route (v. 8). The witnesses converge on a portrait of energetic folly — a new king raising the whole kingdom to arms with commendable vigour, a good king lending himself once more to a bad house with commendable but perilous charity, and a campaign launched, as the next verses show, before either had asked a single question of the Lord. The muster is complete; the counsel of God is not yet sought.
7:
The formal message and its wholehearted answer are the verse’s substance, and the witnesses fasten on the striking echo it sounds. Jehoram’s embassy — ‘The king of Moab hath rebelled against me: wilt thou go with me against Moab to battle?’ — draws from Jehoshaphat the same pledge, word for word, that he had once given Ahab: ‘I am as thou art, my people as thy people, and my horses as thy horses.’ Charles Ellicott marks the repetition precisely: ‘So Ahab asks Jehoshaphat in 1 Kings 22:4, and he replies as here.’ He even infers common authorship, that ‘the present section was originally composed by the same hand’ as the war-narratives of Ahab’s reign. Adam Clarke reads the same echo devotionally — ‘Jehoshaphat maintained the same friendly intercourse with the son, as he did with the father’ (1 Kings 22:4).

If the alliance is a fault, the witnesses are careful to weigh its motives fairly. Ellicott recalls that Jehoshaphat ‘assented, in spite of the prophetic censures of his alliance with Ahab and Ahaziah’ (2 Chronicles 19:2; 2 Chronicles 20:37), and offers the likely reason: ‘he was anxious to inflict further punishment on the Moabites for their inroad into Judah’ (2 Chronicles 20:1), and to prevent its recurrence. He would also render the request more exactly — not ‘against Moab to battle’ but ‘into Moab to the war.’

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley lays out the whole calculus of a just and prudent war, and it is the fullest defence of Jehoshaphat any witness gives. He joined, Wesley reasons, ‘because the war was just in itself, and convenient for Jehoshaphat’: just, ‘that revolters should be chastised, lest the examples should pass into his dominions, and the Edomites be encouraged to revolt from him, as they did from his son’; and convenient, ‘that the Moabites should be humbled, who had invaded his land before this time’ (2 Chronicles 20:1), ‘and might do so again if they were not brought low.’ Between Ellicott’s literary eye, Clarke’s warm approval, and Wesley’s careful statecraft, the witnesses agree on the double truth the verse holds: Jehoshaphat’s readiness is at once the fruit of a generous, forgiving temper and the repetition of an entanglement the prophets had already condemned — a good man’s virtue and a good man’s blind spot bound up in the very same words.
8:
A single question of strategy — ‘Which way shall we go up?’ — and a single answer, ‘The way through the wilderness of Edom,’ open the door to the whole disaster that follows, and the witnesses read the choice with a soldier’s eye. John Gill frames the decision as Jehoram consulting Jehoshaphat ‘which was the best way to take to the land of Moab, whether the shortest way, over Jordan, or some other.’ Adam Clarke gives the immediate reason for the roundabout route: ‘because he expected the king of Edom to join them,’ Edom ‘being tributary to Judah’ and so ‘obliged’ to send its forces.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the map in full. The allies ‘might cross the Jordan, and attack the northern frontier of Moab, or they might round the southern end of the Dead Sea, and invade Moab from the side of Edom.’ The northern way ‘was the shortest route for both kings,’ but it had grave drawbacks: ‘Moab’s strongest defences were on the north frontier, and the allies would be liable to attacks from the Syrians in Ramoth-gilead’ (2 Kings 8:28). The longer southern road commended itself on several counts — it let Jehoshaphat ‘march as far as might be within his own territory,’ pick up ‘a contingent from Edom, which was at this time subject to him’ (1 Kings 22:48), ‘and perhaps to hold it in check’; and, not least, ‘the Moabites were less likely to be on their guard on the southern border, which was more difficult of access.’ It was, on paper, the shrewder plan.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry catches the human logic that made three kings seem safer than two: to go ‘round through the wilderness of Edom, that they might take the king of Edom and his forces along with them,’ reasoning that ‘if two be better than one, much more will not a threefold cord be easily broken’ (compare Ecclesiastes 4:12). Yet the witnesses let the irony stand plain. The route chosen for its cunning — its cover, its extra ally, its safe passage through friendly ground — is the very route that will strand the whole army seven days deep in a waterless wilderness. Human strategy reaches its limit exactly where Jehoshaphat’s later question about a prophet will begin: the best-laid road of three kings runs, without God consulted, straight into a trap of thirst. The witnesses agree that the plan was wise and that wisdom was not enough.
9:
The threefold cord frays at once. ‘They fetched a compass of seven days’ journey: and there was no water for the host, and for the cattle that followed them.’ The witnesses divide their attention between the geography of the blunder and its lesson. Adam Clarke states the double truth bluntly: the circuit round ‘the southern part of the Dead Sea’ was meant ‘to surprise the Moabites,’ but ‘it appears their journey was ill planned, as they at last got into a country in which it was impossible to obtain water, and they were brought in consequence to the utmost extremity.’

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the route step by step. Keil and Delitzsch take the army through ‘the deep rocky valley of the Ahsy,’ the natural corridor from southern Judah up toward Moab, and mark the moment the plan unravels — after seven days’ marching the host and its beasts stand without water. Charles Ellicott corrects even the punctuation of the older text: it should read ‘there was not water for the army and for the cattle which followed them,’ ‘the cattle’ being ‘the herds and flocks for the maintenance of the army’ (compare the idiom of Judges 5:15). He judges that ‘the confederates appear to have lost their way among the mountains of Seir,’ were ‘greatly delayed by the cattle,’ and that ‘the distress began after the Edomite contingent had joined.’ They had marched ‘through the deep, rocky glen of El-Ahsy,’ expecting the water ‘usually’ found there ‘even in the dry season’ — ‘but on this occasion the water failed.’

The wesleyan-arminian voices add the causes. John Wesley explains the seven days ‘because they made a great army, which could move but slowly; and they fetched a greater compass than was usual, for some advantage which they expected by it,’ while the drought was ‘a frequent want in those parts,’ now ‘increased by the extraordinary heat and dryness of the season.’ Wesley also settles the standing puzzle of the ‘king of Edom,’ who was in truth ‘the vice-roy under Jehoshaphat’ (1 Kings 22:47), ‘called king, because that word is sometimes used for any prince or chief ruler.’

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the marrow of the verse, and the witnesses converge on his point. The danger ‘ought to have been considered before they ventured a march through the wilderness — the same wilderness, or very near it, where their ancestors wanted water’ (Numbers 20:2). ‘God suffers his people, by their own improvidence, to bring themselves into distress, that the wisdom, power, and goodness of his providence may be glorified in their relief.’ And Henry marvels at the humbling instrument: ‘What is more cheap and common than water? It is drink to every beast of the field’ (Psalms 104:11); ‘yet the want of it will soon humble and ruin kings and armies.’ So the witnesses agree — three crowned heads and a whole host, undone not by Moab’s sword but by an empty streambed, brought low that God might have room to show his hand.
10:
Distress finds its voice in the king of Israel’s despairing cry: ‘Alas! that the Lord hath called these three kings together, to deliver them into the hand of Moab!’ The witnesses hear in it a revealing self-deception, and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry names it exactly. Jehoram ‘sadly lamented the present distress’; yet ‘it was he that had called these kings together, and yet he charges it upon Providence, and reflects upon that as unkind.’ Henry seals it with the proverb that fits like a key: ‘Thus the foolishness of man perverteth his way, and then his heart fretteth against the Lord’ (Proverbs 19:3). The king who plotted the route now arraigns heaven for the ditch he dug himself.

The reformed John Gill reads the same evasion in the man’s psychology: he ‘was the more concerned, as he was the principal who had drawn the other kings into this affair, though he throws it upon the Lord and his providence.’ Adam Clarke hears something darker still in the words — not merely evasion but the language of a guilty conscience confessing more than it means: ‘This is a Divine judgment; God has judicially blinded us, and permitted us to take this journey to our destruction.’ In Clarke’s reading Jehoram half-knows the truth, that the calamity is judicial, even while he shifts the blame for it.

The grammatical-historical commentators watch the divine strategy in the trouble. Charles Ellicott, attentive as ever to the letter, notes that the emphatic Hebrew particle introducing the assertion and the word ‘together’ are best left untranslated, tightening the cry to its bare complaint. Keil and Delitzsch read the whole scene as designed for the king’s good: the aim was ‘still further to humble the king of Israel, who was already bowed down by the trouble, and to produce some salutary fruit of repentance in his heart’ — the very purpose Elisha’s stern reception will serve. Here the witnesses reach their quiet agreement. The verse is a mirror of the natural heart under judgment: quick to feel the pain, quick to fault God, slow to own the sin that caused it. Jehoram’s ‘Alas’ is the cry of every man who blames Providence for the harvest of his own counsels; and God, meaning mercy even in the misery, lets the thirst press until it drives a proud king, and his better companion, to ask at last for a prophet of the Lord.
11:
The turn of the whole chapter comes when Jehoshaphat, not Jehoram, speaks: ‘Is there not here a prophet of the Lord, that we may inquire of the Lord by him?’ The witnesses hear in the two kings’ opposite instincts the difference between faith and despair. Charles Ellicott notes that this is the very question Jehoshaphat asked in Ahab’s camp (1 Kings 22:7), even to the idiom ‘from with him,’ which he counts ‘a mark of the same hand’; and he draws the contrast sharply — Jehoshaphat ‘is for seeking Jehovah through a prophet, in contrast with Jehoram, who at once despairs’ (compare Amos 5:4).

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agree on the gentle rebuke folded into the good king’s piety: ‘this the good king should have done before he set out, but had neglected it; however, it was not too late.’ Wesley presses it home — he ‘should have asked before, when they first undertook the expedition, as he did in a like case’ (1 Kings 22:5) — ‘and for that neglect he now suffers; but better late than never: his affliction brings him to the remembrance of his former sin, and present duty.’ The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry frames the same lesson memorably: ‘Good men are sometimes remiss and forgetful, and neglect their duty till necessity and affliction drive them to it.’

Then the hidden mercy surfaces. A nameless officer answers, ‘Here is Elisha the son of Shaphat, which poured water on the hands of Elijah.’ Adam Clarke glosses the phrase — Elisha ‘was his constant and confidential servant’ — and Ellicott explains the vivid Oriental image, ‘the servant pouring water from a ewer on his master’s hands to wash them.’ That such a jewel lay unnoticed in the camp is, to the witnesses, the marrow of the verse. Henry marvels that Elisha ‘should follow the camp’ as ‘a volunteer, unasked, unobserved, and in no post of honour at all,’ in such obscurity ‘that none of the kings knew they had such a jewel in the treasures of their camp.’ ‘Thus,’ he writes, ‘does God anticipate his people with the blessings of his goodness, and provide his oracles for those that provide them not for themselves.’ Wesley says the same: ‘probably it was by a special direction from God that Elisha followed them, unasked, unobserved. Thus does God prevent us with the blessings of his goodness.’

Ellicott and Henry both note the further wonder that ‘a servant of the king of Israel knew of his being there when the king himself did not’ — a hidden believer, like Obadiah in Ahab’s house (1 Kings 18:3), keeping account of the prophet the court had lost track of. And Henry draws from Elisha’s old office the enduring proverb: ‘He that will be great, let him learn to minister; he that will rise high, let him begin low.’ The witnesses do not debate; they agree that God had gone before the panic with the remedy, planting his prophet in the ranks and his prophet’s friend at the king’s elbow, so that faith had only to ask.
12:
Jehoshaphat’s verdict on the servant’s report — ‘The word of the Lord is with him’ — settles the matter, and the three kings go down to the prophet’s tent. The witnesses read the scene as a quiet exaltation of humility. John Gill explains how Jehoshaphat could speak so confidently: he ‘concluded’ it ‘from his being a servant and disciple of Elijah, whom he succeeded in his office, and of whom he had, doubtless, heard.’ Adam Clarke compresses the phrase to its plain sense — ‘he has the gift of prophecy.’

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the touches of a historian. Jehoshaphat spoke as he did ‘either on the ground of Elijah’s reputation, or because the news of Elisha’s succession had already reached Judah’; and Ellicott notices the fitting play of names — ‘Shaphat and Jehoshaphat are identical,’ both meaning ‘He judgeth’ — the son of Shaphat vindicated before the king whose own name confesses that ‘Jah judgeth.’ The kings ‘went down to him’ from ‘the royal tents, which were probably pitched on an eminence, so as to overlook the camp,’ and the very direction of their going carries the point: ‘the three kings go to consult the prophet as persons of ordinary station might do. This shows the estimation in which he was held’ — or, as Keil adds, ‘they were humbled by misfortune.’ John Wesley agrees they went rather than sent ‘that by giving him this honour, they might engage him to give them his utmost assistance.’

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the wonder of the reversal into his happiest vein. Jehoshaphat’s reverence was such ‘that he would condescend to visit him in his own person and not send for him’; the other two ‘were moved by the straits they were in to make their court to the prophet.’ And so, Henry writes, ‘he that humbled himself was thus exalted, and looked great, when three kings came to knock at his door, and beg his assistance’ — pointing to the promise that the faithful shall yet be honoured before their adversaries (Revelation 3:9). The witnesses do not divide. They see in the picture of three crowned men trooping to a prophet’s tent the standing truth of God’s economy: the man who once poured water on another’s hands is sought out by kings who cannot save themselves, and the honour the court had overlooked is repaid in full when honour is all the court has left to give.
13:
Elisha’s greeting to Jehoram is a rebuke, not a welcome: ‘What have I to do with thee? Get thee to the prophets of thy father, and to the prophets of thy mother.’ The witnesses feel the sting and admire its justice. John Gill hears the prophet refusing fellowship with ‘an idolater’ — ‘I can hold no discourse nor have any conversation with thee.’ Adam Clarke calls it ‘a just, but cutting reproof,’ and notes that the Chaldee and Arabic paraphrases add a softening plea on the king’s behalf, ‘I beseech thee, do not mention our transgressions, but use kindness towards us.’

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley sharpens the dismissal into a taunt: ‘Get thee to the calves, which thou after thy father’s example dost worship; and to the Baals which thy mother yet worshippeth by thy permission; let these idols whom thou worshippest in thy prosperity, now help thee in thy distress.’ The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies ‘the prophets of thy father’ as ‘the Baal prophets’ of Ahab’s court (1 Kings 18:19) and the false prophets of Jehovah (1 Kings 22:6), and reads Elisha’s ‘sarcasm’ as evidence that ‘the former had not been wholly rooted out’ — Jehoram’s reform, again, exposed as skin-deep. Ellicott also weighs the king’s reply, ‘Nay’: it means ‘Say not so,’ or ‘Repulse me not’ (as in Ruth 1:13); and the plea ‘these three kings’ leans on the word ‘three,’ as if to ‘rouse compassion by the magnitude of the imminent disaster’ — not one guilty man only, but two better allies who would perish with him.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the exchange its theological edge. Elisha, he says, ‘was not imposed upon, as Jehoshaphat was, by his partial and hypocritical reformation,’ but knew that ‘though he had put away the image of Baal, Baal’s prophets were still dear to him.’ The prophet’s message is the old summons of the judges’ book — ‘Go to the gods whom you have served’ (Judges 10:14) — and of Ezekiel’s rebuke to idols set up in the heart (Ezekiel 14:3): ‘The world and the flesh have ruled you; let them help you; why should God be enquired of by you?’ Yet the witnesses note the mercy inside the severity. Jehoram bears the plain dealing ‘patiently,’ no longer caring ‘for the prophets of Baal,’ but coming as ‘a humble suitor to the God of Israel.’ The reproof has done its work: the man who blamed Providence now owns himself unworthy and pleads that his companions not be ruined for his sake. The prophet’s hard word, the witnesses agree, is aimed not to crush but to strip away the last false refuge, that a proud king in his extremity might have nowhere to look but up.
14:
The oath that follows is one of the boldest a prophet ever swore before a king: ‘As the Lord of hosts liveth, before whom I stand, surely, were it not that I regard the presence of Jehoshaphat the king of Judah, I would not look toward thee, nor see thee.’ The witnesses weigh both the awful reverence toward God and the withering contempt toward Jehoram. John Gill reads ‘before whom I stand’ as the prophet’s whole vocation — the God ‘whose minister and prophet he was, to whom he prayed, and whose service he was ready to perform.’ Charles Ellicott confirms the sense: ‘before whom I stand’ means ‘as a minister’ (1 Kings 17:1; 1 Kings 18:15), and ‘regard the presence’ is, literally, ‘lift the face’ (Genesis 19:21) — Elisha will lift his face toward the camp for one man’s sake only.

That one man is the crux, and the witnesses agree on why the prophet distinguishes so sharply between the two kings. Adam Clarke puts it in a line: Jehoshaphat ‘worshipped the true God; Jehoram was an idolater.’ Ellicott says Jehoshaphat ‘is accepted because of his faithful dependence on Jehovah’ (v. 3 having just recorded that Jehoram ‘still maintained or tolerated the cultus of Bethel and Dan’). John Wesley makes it warm and personal — Jehoshaphat, ‘whom I reverence and love for his piety’ — and draws the comfortable inference: ‘It is good being with those who have God’s favour, and the love of his people. Wicked men often fare the better for the friendship and society of good men.’ Jehoram, in this reckoning, is spared for his companion’s sake; the ungodly king is answered only because a godly king stands beside him.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry brings out the double posture the moment requires of a prophet who is also a subject. Elisha ‘as a subject will honour’ the king, ‘but as a prophet he will cause him to know his iniquity.’ Jehoram ‘is to be respected as a prince, but as a wicked man he is a vile person, and is to be condemned’ (Psalms 15:4); for those armed with such an ‘extraordinary commission’ it was fitting, ‘though not for a common person,’ to say even to a king, ‘Thou art wicked’ (Job 34:18). Keil and Delitzsch read the whole harsh speech as still remedial, meant to bow a king ‘already bowed down by the trouble’ lower yet. The witnesses reach a united judgment: the prophet’s fearlessness rests wholly on the God before whom he stands, and the only thing that shields the guilty king from being ignored altogether is the presence of a believing friend — a striking testimony to how much the ungodly owe, often unknowing, to the righteous in their company.
15:
Before the oracle comes a remarkable request: ‘But now bring me a minstrel. And it came to pass, when the minstrel played, that the hand of the Lord came upon him.’ The witnesses agree, almost as one, on what the music was for — to still a prophet’s ruffled spirit so that God could speak. John Gill explains that Elisha called for ‘a piper, a man that knows how to play upon the harp,’ ‘to allay his passion, and compose his spirits, ruffled at the sight of Jehoram, and to fit him to receive prophetic inspiration’; he even guesses the music was of the grave, settling kind ‘the Greeks call harmony.’ John Wesley says the same in his own key: the minstrel was called ‘that his mind, which had been disturbed at the sight of wicked Jehoram, might be composed, and that he might be excited to more fervent prayer, whereby he was prepared to receive the prophetic inspiration.’ And Wesley draws the abiding rule: ‘Those that desire communion with God must keep their spirits quiet and serene. All hurry of spirit, and all turbulent passions, make us unfit for divine visitations.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott together supply the striking classical parallel. Clarke recalls that ‘Elisha’s mind was considerably irritated and grieved’ — by the recent tragedy of the youths at Bethel and by ‘the presence of the idolatrous king of Israel’ — and so ‘called for Divine psalmody, that it might calm his spirits, and render him more susceptible of the prophetic influence.’ ‘To be able to discern the voice of God,’ he reasons, ‘it is necessary that the mind be calm, and the passions all in harmony, under the direction of reason.’ Ellicott agrees that ‘composure and serenity of soul were essential, if the prophet was to hear the voice of God within,’ and both cite Cicero’s report that ‘the Pythagoreans were wont to tranquillise their minds’ after strain ‘with harp music and singing’ (compare the band of prophets with harp and pipe in 1 Samuel 10:5). Clarke adds the telling contrast between true and false inspiration: the heathen priests were ‘thrown into violent agitations and contortions by the influence of the demons which possessed them,’ while the prophets of God ‘were in a state of the utmost serenity and composure.’ Ellicott judges ‘the incident a striking mark of the historical truth of the narrative.’

On the closing words all are agreed. Ellicott notes that some manuscripts and the Targum read ‘the Spirit of the Lord’ for ‘the hand of the Lord’ (compare 1 Kings 18:46), while Wesley insists the phrase marks a gift, not a talent: ‘the spirit of prophecy, so called, to note that it was no natural nor acquired virtue inherent in him, but a singular gift of God, given to whom and when he pleased.’ The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry rounds it off: the sweet singing of God’s praises, ‘as David had appointed,’ would ‘cheer his spirits, and settle his mind,’ and so, refreshed, ‘the hand of the Lord came upon him.’ The witnesses reach one pastoral conclusion — that the calm of a quieted, worshipping heart is the appointed antechamber to the voice of God.
16:
The oracle opens with a command that tests obedience before it gives relief: ‘Thus saith the Lord, Make this valley full of ditches.’ The witnesses dwell on the strange condition — that men dying of thirst must first dig — and on the bare, springless way the water is promised. John Gill reads the purpose plainly: the pits were dug ‘that they might be ready to receive large quantities of water, sufficient for the whole army and cattle, when it came.’ Charles Ellicott supplies the topography: the word is not a green ‘valley’ but a nahal, a ‘wady, torrent-bed, gully’ — Thenius identifies it with ‘the brook Zered’ (Deuteronomy 2:13), the present Wady el-Ahsy — and the ‘ditches’ are, literally, ‘pits, pits,’ on the doubling-idiom of ‘wells, wells of bitumen’ in Genesis 14:10. The pits ‘were to gather the water, which otherwise would soon have run away in the bed of the torrent’ (compare the dry cisterns of Jeremiah 14:3); and Ellicott judges ‘the style of the oracle stamped with the liveliness and originality of historic truth.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke pictures the engineering. The nachal ‘may be translated brook’; ‘there probably was a river here, but it was now dry,’ and the prophet ‘desires that they would enlarge the channel, and cut out various canals from it, and reservoirs, where water might be collected.’ Clarke even suspects a further design — the pits ‘made so wide that the reflection of the sun’s rays from this water might be the means of confounding and destroying the Moabites,’ anticipating the deadly optical illusion of the next morning.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the command as the first clause of a mounting promise: to give water is ‘too little for Jehovah,’ who ‘will also give Moab into your hand,’ and they note that the coming devastation — felled trees, stopped springs, fields marred with stones — carries a deliberate allusion to the laws of siege in Deuteronomy 20:19-20.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds the verse’s heart in its odd demand. ‘To try their faith and obedience, he bids them make the valley full of ditches to receive the water. Those that expect God’s blessings must prepare room for them, dig the pools for the rain to fill, as they did in the valley of Baca, and so made even that a well’ (Psalms 84:6). The witnesses converge on this: the God who needs no rain-cloud still asks his people to dig, that provision might meet preparation, and the blessing find a place made ready to hold it. Faith empties itself in labour on the bare promise, and heaven fills what faith has hollowed out.
17:
The promise grows more wonderful as it grows more specific: ‘Ye shall not see wind, neither shall ye see rain; yet that valley shall be filled with water, that ye may drink, both ye, and your cattle, and your beasts.’ The witnesses fasten on the marvel that the water is pledged without any of its usual heralds. John Gill spells out the absent signs — they would ‘neither perceive the south wind blow, which usually brings rain, nor clouds gathering in the heavens, as portending it, nor any falling from thence.’ The relief is severed from every natural cause a watching army might read in the sky.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the language. ‘Wind,’ he notes, ‘in the east is the usual precursor of rain’; its absence makes the supply the more astonishing. He catches a fine distinction of pronouns, too: the oracle says ‘that valley’ — ‘the one of which I spoke’ — over against ‘this valley’ ‘in which we are,’ pointing the promise beyond the camp toward the distant channel where the flood would rise. And he separates the beneficiaries with care: ‘your cattle’ (miqneh) are the ‘flocks and herds,’ while the ‘beasts’ (behemah) are ‘probably beasts of burden’ — every living creature in the host, down to the pack-animals, would drink.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the theology the miracle was made to teach. He sets Elisha beside his master: ‘Elijah, by prayer, obtained water out of the clouds’ (as at Carmel in 1 Kings 18), ‘but Elisha fetches it nobody knows whence. The spring of these waters shall be as secret as the head of the Nile.’ The lesson is God’s freedom over his own creation: ‘Ordinarily it is by a plentiful rain that God confirms his inheritance’ (Psalms 68:9), ‘but here it is done without rain.’ ‘God,’ Henry concludes, ‘is not tied to second causes.’ The witnesses reach a single confession: the same Lord who commonly works through wind and cloud can, when he pleases, lay every ordinary means aside and simply give, so that his people learn to trust the Giver and not the signs — to look for water even under a cloudless, windless sky, because the word of promise has gone out.
18:
The oracle now leaps from mercy to abundance: ‘And this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord: he will deliver the Moabites also into your hand.’ The witnesses marvel at the ‘but a light thing’ — that the miracle which would save a perishing army is, to God, the smaller half of what he means to do. John Gill measures the two gifts: ‘to give them such a plenty of water in such an extraordinary manner’ was the lesser; ‘he would do for them what was greater, not only save them from perishing, but’ grant them the victory itself. Charles Ellicott renders the phrase as future — ‘it will be a light thing’ — and points the sweet reversal it contains: that God ‘will deliver the Moabites’ is ‘the contrary of Jehoram’s expectation,’ who had wailed that the Lord had gathered the kings only ‘to deliver them into the hand of Moab’ (v. 10; v. 13). The very words of the king’s despair are taken up and turned inside out; the hand he feared would close on him is the hand that will be filled with his enemies.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes this overflow of grace the verse’s whole message. ‘This is but a light thing,’ he paraphrases; ‘you shall not only be saved from perishing, but shall return in triumph.’ And he lifts it into a doctrine of the divine generosity: ‘As God gives freely to the unworthy, so he gives richly, like himself, more than we are able to ask or think. His grants out-do our requests and expectations.’ The kings had come begging bare survival; God answers with survival and conquest together. Henry’s application reaches to every believer’s prayers: ‘Those that sincerely seek for the dew of God’s grace shall have it, and by it be made more than conquerors’ (Romans 8:37).

The witnesses land as one on the lavishness of God. He does not dole out mercy to the exact measure of our need or our asking; he saves the dying and crowns them victors in a single word, so that what looks to us a great deliverance is to him a light thing, and his least gift outruns our largest hope. The verse becomes, in their hands, a window into the character of the Giver: the God of Elisha is not a reluctant helper wringing out a bare rescue, but a King who, having stooped to fill a ditch with water, thinks it a small matter to hand a kingdom over with it. What the three kings dared not pray for, he freely adds; and the measure of his giving is not their faith but his own royal fulness.
19:
The oracle’s last clause turns to the ruin of Moab — ‘ye shall smite every fenced city, and every choice city, and shall fell every good tree, and stop all wells of water, and mar every good piece of land with stones’ — and here the witnesses meet a real difficulty and genuinely divide over it. The trouble is the felling of fruit-trees, which the law of Moses forbade in siege (Deuteronomy 20:19). The witnesses take three distinct paths through it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott offers the cleanest solution: the verbs are ‘continuative of those in the last verse,’ so that ‘they do not command a course of action, but foretell it’ (compare 2 Kings 8:12-13). Read as prophecy rather than precept, the words describe what the victors would do, not what God enjoined, and the conflict with the law dissolves. Ellicott adds the touches of a scholar — the ‘paronomasia,’ or play on similar sounds, in the Hebrew for ‘fenced city’ and ‘choice city,’ and the poetic force of ‘mar,’ which is ‘literally, make to grieve,’ an ‘unfruitful land’ being said ‘to mourn’ (Isaiah 24:4; Jeremiah 12:4). Keil and Delitzsch supply the alternative reconciliation, taking the words as command but limiting the law’s scope: the prohibition ‘relates to Canaanite territory which the Israelites were to occupy, whereas Moab’s was an enemy’s country, and therefore not to be spared.’

The reformed John Gill canvasses both answers together — the felling ‘seems contrary to the law,’ but ‘that may respect trees belonging to a city when besieged only, or only to Canaanitish cities; or the law was now dispensed with, and that for this time only, to make the punishment of Moab the greater, for their rebellion and other sins.’ The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes the sternest line on the exegesis, insisting the fruit-trees cannot be meant, ‘for this was positively against the law of God,’ and reading the felled trees as only those ‘by which your enemies may serve themselves for fortifications’; he explains the tactics of the rest — to ‘stop all wells of water’ would ‘reduce an enemy’ faster ‘than any thing else’ in those hot lands, and to have each man cast his stone would let ‘such a multitude of men’ ‘completely destroy’ a good field.

Clarke’s fellow wesleyan-arminian John Wesley meets the moral scruple head-on rather than the legal one: ‘if this command seem severe, it must be considered that the Moabites were a very wicked people, perfidious, cruel, implacable enemies to God’s people upon all occasions, and now in a state of rebellion.’ So the witnesses debate the letter — prediction or command, Canaanite law or Moabite exception, timber or orchard — yet close ranks on the substance. Whether foretold or enjoined, the desolation of Moab is a just recompense upon a hardened and rebellious enemy of God’s people, and the very fullness of the coming ruin is itself a measure of how completely the Lord means to keep the ‘light thing’ he has promised.
20:
The word is fulfilled at the appointed hour: ‘it came to pass in the morning, when the meat offering was offered, that, behold, there came water by the way of Edom, and the country was filled with water.’ The witnesses linger on the timing, which none of them thinks accidental. John Gill ties it to Jerusalem, where ‘the meat offering always went along with the daily burnt offering of the lamb’ at break of day. Charles Ellicott confirms that, ‘according to the Talmud, the morning sacrifice was offered in the Temple the moment it became light,’ and calls it ‘a striking coincidence’ ‘that help came to the distressed army just at the hour of morning worship’ (compare 1 Kings 18:36).

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley presses the coincidence into doctrine. The morning sacrifice ‘was attended with the solemn prayers of God’s people,’ and ‘at this time Elisha joined his prayers with the prayers of God’s people, especially those at Jerusalem.’ More boldly, Wesley finds a polemical purpose in the hour: ‘God chose this time to answer their prayers, and to work this miracle, that thereby he might determine the controversy between the Israelites and the Jews about the place and manner of worship, and give a public testimony from heaven for the Jews, and against the Israelites’ — the water arriving at the very moment of the Jerusalem altar’s service, and not at Bethel’s. Ellicott hears a like note, that ‘this allusion to the law of Exodus 29:38 may be an indirect hit at the northern kingdom.’

On the manner of the miracle the grammatical-historical Ellicott is the most careful. The text reads ‘water was coming from the way of Edom’; ‘it would seem that a sudden storm of rain had fallen on the mountains of Seir, at some distance from the camp’ — Josephus says three days’ march off — ‘and the water found its natural outlet in the dry wady.’ Yet Ellicott refuses to let this dissolve the wonder: against those who call the explanation ‘superfluous,’ he answers that ‘there are different kinds of miracle, and, in the present instance, the miraculous element is visible in the prophet’s prediction of the coming help, and in the coincidence of the natural phenomena with the needs of the Israelites.’ Adam Clarke is content to call it simply ‘altogether miraculous, for there was neither wind nor rain, nor any other natural means by which it could be supplied.’

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the threads and adds a second gift folded into the first: the flood ‘did them double service’ — it ‘relieved their armies, which were ready to perish,’ and it would soon ‘deceive their enemies, who were ready to triumph.’ He notes that God had once ‘answered Daniel’s prayer just at the time of the evening sacrifice’ (Daniel 9:21), ‘for he will acknowledge his own institutions.’ The witnesses reach a shared confession: the God ‘who commands all the waters, both above and beneath the firmament,’ sent them at the hour of worship, honouring the despised daily sacrifice and binding the rescue of an army to the prayers of his people at his altar.
21:
The scene shifts across the valley to the Moabite side, where the news of the invasion has raised the whole nation to arms: ‘when all the Moabites heard that the kings were come up to fight against them, they gathered all that were able to put on armour, and upward, and stood in the border.’ John Gill identifies ‘the kings’ as the three allies, ‘of Israel, Judah, and Edom,’ whose combined advance had reached Moabite ears. Keil and Delitzsch read it as a general mobilization: ‘on hearing the report of the march of the allied kings, Moab had raised all the men that were capable of bearing arms, and stationed them on the frontier.’

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the graphic Hebrew. The verbs are pluperfect — the Moabites ‘had heard’ and ‘had gathered themselves,’ or, more exactly, ‘had been summoned, called together’ (as in Judges 7:23). The phrase ‘all that were able to put on armour’ is, literally, ‘from every one girding on a girdle, and upwards’ — ‘all of adult age, all who could bear arms.’ It was, Ellicott says, ‘a levy en masse of the male population for the defence of the country,’ every man old enough to buckle a belt turned out to the border, where they ‘had taken their stand on the frontier.’

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry catches the confidence with which they took their post. The Moabites were ‘ready to give the Israelites a warm reception,’ Henry writes, ‘promising themselves that it would be easy dealing with an army fatigued by so long a march through the wilderness of Edom.’ Here the witnesses let the dramatic irony do its work. The whole strength of Moab is drawn up in good order along the frontier, armed, watchful, and sure of an easy prey — and it is precisely this readiness that will betray them. For the same men who ‘stood in the border’ to fight a disciplined battle will, at sunrise, break ranks and rush headlong for plunder at the sight of what they take for blood. The witnesses set the picture before us without moralizing further, content to show a nation mustering its last man to defend itself, all unaware that its danger lies not in the enemy’s sword but in its own eyes and its own greed, which the God of Israel is about to turn against it.
22:
The trap springs with the dawn: ‘they rose up early in the morning, and the sun shone upon the water, and the Moabites saw the water on the other side as red as blood.’ The witnesses reconstruct the fatal illusion with care. John Gill notes they ‘rose up early’ for a soldier’s reason — ‘to watch the motions of their enemies, and be upon their guard.’ Charles Ellicott sets the optics precisely: the Moabite camp lay ‘on the frontier mountains,’ and ‘the sun rose behind’ them, so that its low red light struck the flooded valley across from them — ‘the red sunrise tinged the water with the same colour.’ He reads the clause ‘the sun shone upon the water’ as a parenthesis, ‘now the sun had risen upon the water,’ and hears a possible pun in the word for ‘red,’ adom, glancing at ‘the red earth of the locality’ (Edom), which ‘would further redden the water.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains why seasoned soldiers were so easily fooled. It ‘might have been an optical deception,’ of a kind Clarke says he has himself witnessed; and the Moabites ‘had never seen that valley full of water, and therefore did not suspect that their eyes deceived them, but took it for the blood of the confederate hosts.’ They had a precedent ready to hand — the memory of enemies who had destroyed one another, ‘as the Midianites had formerly done’ (Judges 7:22) and the Philistines lately (1 Samuel 14:20).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weaves the strands into a study of self-deception. They ‘conceited it was blood because they knew the valley to be dry, and, there having been no rain, could not imagine it should be water.’ The very miracle that saved Israel — water where water could not be — became the snare of Moab, who reasoned soundly from a false premise. Henry even suspects the sky lent its aid: ‘probably the sky was red and lowering, a presage of foul weather that day’ (Matthew 16:3). The witnesses reach a sobering agreement. God did not deceive the Moabites; he let them deceive themselves, permitting their own eyes, their own confidence, and their own knowledge of the dry valley to conspire against them. The wonder that was life to the believing army was death to the presuming enemy — the same water, read by faith as God’s gift and by greed as an omen of plunder.
23:
The illusion completes itself in a chain of eager inferences: ‘they said, This is blood: the kings are surely slain, and they have smitten one another: now therefore, Moab, to the spoil.’ The witnesses trace the reasoning link by link and find it plausible at every step but the first. John Gill notes how quickly assumption hardened into certainty — ‘they were very confident of it, having no notion of water, there having been no rain for some time, and perhaps it was not usual to see water at any time in this place.’

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott shows why the deadly guess seemed so reasonable. ‘The kings are surely slain’ means ‘have fought with, or destroyed, one another’; and ‘the supposition was not improbable,’ for ‘confederates of different races not seldom had been known to fall out among themselves’ (Judges 7:22; 2 Chronicles 20:23). In this case the odds seemed especially good — ‘the old enmity of Edom towards Israel, and the suppressed jealousies between Israel and Judah, made such a result very likely,’ and ‘the Moabites would know also that the wady had been waterless, so that their mistake was natural.’ Then, Ellicott says, ‘when once their instinct for plunder was aroused they did not stop to think, but with a wild cry of “Moab, to the spoil!” they rushed in disorder upon the Israelite camp.’ Adam Clarke agrees they ‘came on in a disorderly manner, and fell an easy prey to their enemies.’

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lays bare the psychology beneath the blunder, and it is his sharpest word on the chapter. The conclusion was not forced on the Moabites; it was the conclusion they wished for. These ‘were the gradual suggestions of some sanguine spirits among them,’ and ‘the rest, being desirous it should be so, were forward to believe it was so.’ Henry crystallizes it in the Latin proverb, ‘Quod volumus facile credimus — What we wish we readily believe.’ And he presses it to its terrible end: ‘Thus those that are to be destroyed are first deceived’ (Revelation 20:8), ‘and none are so effectually deceived as those that deceive themselves.’ The witnesses converge on the lesson without needing to labour it: greed reads the evidence as it hopes, calls a field of water a field of blood, and runs shouting toward the very ruin it imagines it is escaping. Self-interest is the surest false prophet, and the plunder they ran to seize became the grave they ran to fill.
24:
The plunderers meet an army instead of a corpse-strewn field: ‘when they came to the camp of Israel, the Israelites rose up and smote the Moabites, so that they fled before them: but they went forward smiting the Moabites, even in their country.’ The witnesses mark the completeness of the rout and the disorder that made it possible. John Gill pictures the Moabites arriving ‘not in an orderly regular manner, in rank and file, as an army should march, but in a confused manner, every one striving who should get thither first, and have the largest share of the booty’ — a mob, not a force, and so undone the moment it was resisted.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the Israelites ‘smote the Moabites, who were unprepared for resistance,’ and works patiently over a tangled Hebrew text. The written form reads ‘and he went into it’ — the land of Moab — ‘and smote Moab,’ which Ellicott judges ‘better than the Hebrew margin’ and the smoother versions; the original sense is likely that of the Septuagint, ‘they entered the country, destroying as they went on.’ The pursuit did not stop at the camp’s edge but rolled forward over the frontier and into Moab itself.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the spring of the victory, and it is faith. The Moabites ‘rushed carelessly into the camp of Israel, to plunder it, but were undeceived when it was too late.’ The Israelites, by contrast, were ‘animated by the assurances Elisha had given them of victory’ — the prophet’s word that God would ‘deliver the Moabites also into your hand’ (v. 18) — and so ‘fell upon them with the utmost fury, routed them, and pursued them into their own country.’ The witnesses reach a plain and bracing agreement. One host came on in the strength of a greedy delusion and broke at the first blow; the other rose up in the strength of a divine promise and could not be stopped. The difference between the two was not numbers or valour but the word of God: Moab fought for spoil and lost everything; Israel fought on a promise and possessed the land. Faith in the sure word of the prophet turned a thirst-worn, half-dead army into a flood of conquerors.
25:
The devastation foretold in the oracle is now carried out to the letter: ‘they beat down the cities, and on every good piece of land cast every man his stone, and filled it; and they stopped all the wells of water, and felled all the good trees: only in Kir-haraseth left they the stones thereof; howbeit the slingers went about it, and smote it.’ The witnesses first note how exactly the ruin answers the prophecy. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott both point back to the word of Elisha (v. 19): ‘all this as Elisha foretold.’ Ellicott renders the verbs as describing ‘what happened again and again’ — ‘the cities they would overthrow,’ ‘every good plot they would cast each man his stone, and fill it,’ ‘every fountain they would stop, and every good tree they would fell’ — a systematic, repeated demolition rolling across the whole land. John Gill sums the physical work: they ‘demolished the walls of them, and houses in them, wherever they came.’

The one place that halts the tide is the capital, and the witnesses gather round its name. Ellicott reads the difficult clause ‘until one left her stones in Kir-hareseth’ as meaning that ‘the work of destruction stopped before the walls of this, the principal stronghold of the country’ — everywhere else ‘the invaders had not left one stone upon another,’ but here the walls held. He identifies the city with the ‘Kir-moab’ of Isaiah 15:1 and the ‘Kir-heres’ of Isaiah 16:11, still called ‘Kerak of Moab,’ standing ‘upon a steep cliff of chalk.’ Adam Clarke agrees it was ‘the royal city of the Moabites, and, as we learn from Scripture, exceedingly strong’ (Isaiah 16:7), ‘so that it is probable the confederate armies could not easily reduce it.’

On the siege of that last citadel the wesleyan-arminian voices work out the tactics. Clarke pictures ‘the slingers’ going ‘about the wall, and smiting all the men that appeared on it, while no doubt the besieging army was employed in sapping the foundations.’ John Wesley reads the ‘stones’ left in Kir-haraseth as ‘the walls and buildings of this city only,’ spared while ‘their whole country’ was ‘destroyed,’ and takes the slingers as those ‘who slung great stones against the walls to break them down,’ making ‘breaches in the walls by which they might enter the city.’ Ellicott adds that the slingers ‘surrounded it’ and ‘shot at the men on the walls with deadly effect.’ The witnesses reach a quiet unanimity: point by point, city by city, tree by tree, the ruin matches the prophet’s words, until Moab is stripped to a single embattled rock — a standing proof that what God speaks by his prophet, his providence performs to the last stone.
26:
Driven to the wall, the king of Moab makes a soldier’s last gamble: ‘when the king of Moab saw that the battle was too sore for him, he took with him seven hundred men that drew swords, to break through even unto the king of Edom: but they could not.’ The witnesses read it as the desperate sortie of a doomed garrison. John Gill sets the scene of extremity — ‘the siege was so close, the slingers or engineers did so much execution, that he saw the city would soon be taken.’ Charles Ellicott agrees ‘the garrison was giving way under the destructive fire of the slingers,’ and weighs the king’s choice of target: he aimed at the king of Edom’s quarter ‘because the Edomite contingent seemed to be the most vulnerable point in the allied army, or because he hoped that these unwilling allies of Israel would allow him to escape through their ranks.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fills in the tactics and the reasoning. The seven hundred ‘were no doubt the choice of all his troops’; ‘afraid of being hemmed up and perhaps taken,’ the king ‘made a desperate sortie in order to regain the open country,’ reckoning ‘the quarter of the Edomites was weakest, or less carefully guarded.’ But the attempt collapsed: ‘they were so warmly received by the king of Edom that they failed in the attempt, and were driven back into the city.’ Clarke marks the fatal consequence — ‘hence he was led to that desperate act mentioned in the following verse’ (v. 27).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry admires the sortie even as he records its failure: the king ‘attempted that which was bold and brave,’ striking at Edom, ‘who, being but a mercenary in this expedition, would not, he hoped, make any great resistance if vigorously attacked.’ Yet ‘it would not do; even the king of Edom proved too hard for him, and obliged him to retire.’ The witnesses let the verse stand as the last rung on a descending ladder. Every ordinary avenue of escape is now closed — the walls breached, the field lost, the sword-charge thrown back — and a cornered king who has exhausted the resources of courage and strategy is left with only one door still open to him, the darkest of all. The failed sally of this verse is the necessary preface to the horror of the next: it is when brave men run out of brave options that they turn to monstrous ones.
27:
The chapter closes in horror and mystery: ‘then he took his eldest son that should have reigned in his stead, and offered him for a burnt offering upon the wall. And there was great indignation against Israel: and they departed from him, and returned to their own land.’ The witnesses agree on the deed and divide, honestly, over its baffling result. On the deed they are of one mind. John Gill settles whose son it was — not, as some rabbis fancied, the captured heir of Edom, but ‘the king of Moab’s own son and heir to the crown.’ Keil and Delitzsch explain the intent: ‘in his desperation he took his first-born son’ and ‘offered him as a sacrifice upon the wall, i.e., in the sight of the besiegers,’ not to the God of Israel ‘but to his own god Chemosh, to procure help from him by appeasing his wrath’ — the heathen way of buying off calamity with ‘human sacrifices.’ Charles Ellicott finds the same practice condemned in the prophet’s cry, ‘Shall I give my firstborn for my transgressions?’ (Micah 6:7), and among Israel’s own apostasies (2 Chronicles 33:6; Psalms 106:37-39).

The crux is the ‘great indignation against Israel,’ and here the schools frankly part. The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the phrase’s usual force: ‘and great wrath fell upon Israel,’ an expression that ‘always denotes a visitation of Divine wrath’ (2 Chronicles 19:10; 2 Chronicles 24:18). But why should wrath fall on Israel, ‘upon the involuntary cause rather than the voluntary agents’? Ellicott’s own answer is startling: he takes it that ‘the object of the dreadful expiation was attained, and that the wrath of Chemosh fell upon the Hebrew alliance’ — for ‘belief in the supremacy of Jehovah did not hinder ancient Israel from admitting the real existence and potency of foreign deities’ (Numbers 21:29; Judges 11:24) — and he reads this ‘peculiar conception’ as ‘a token of the antiquity of the record.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke resists that reading and offers gentler ones: either ‘the Lord was displeased with them for driving things to such an extremity,’ or ‘the surrounding nations held them in abomination on account’ of it, or ‘they were so terrified themselves at this most horrid sacrifice’ that ‘they immediately raised the siege and departed.’ The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes the last line: the indignation was against Israel ‘for driving him to this extremity, whereupon they raised the siege and returned.’ Clarke and Henry both hear the deed as a window into the darkness of false religion — a god who, ‘being a devil, delighted in blood’ — against which Clarke sets the Christian’s opposite Lord: ‘the Son of man is not come to destroy men’s lives, but to save them’ (Luke 9:56).

So the witnesses end divided on the mechanism and united on the wisdom. Whether the ‘wrath’ be God’s displeasure, the horror of the watching world, or (with Ellicott) a concession to how ancient Israel conceived the powers of the nations, all draw the same practical lesson Henry states best: ‘It is well not to urge the worst of men to extremities; we should rather leave them to the judgment of God.’ A victory pressed to the point of driving a man to sacrifice his son is a victory that recoils; and the campaign that began with three kings and a prophet’s promise of triumph ends with the allies retiring from a smoking wall, the mystery of divine providence left, as so often, deeper than the commentators can sound.
